description: The smallest and simplest Kubernetes object. A Pod represents a set of running containers on a cluster node, sharing network namespace and storage volumes.
example_use: Run a single-instance application, sidecar containers, or a debug shell alongside an app.

description: Manages a ReplicaSet to ensure a specified number of Pod replicas are running at any time. Supports declarative updates, rollbacks, and rolling update strategies.
example_use: Deploy a stateless web application with multiple replicas and rolling updates.

description: Ensures that a specified number of Pod replicas are running at any time. Usually managed by a Deployment rather than created directly.
example_use: Maintained automatically by a Deployment; rarely created or managed directly.

description: Manages stateful applications. Provides stable network identities, stable persistent storage, and ordered deployment and scaling for Pods.
example_use: Deploy databases (MySQL, PostgreSQL, Cassandra) or distributed systems requiring stable identities.

description: Ensures that all (or some) nodes run a copy of a Pod. As nodes are added to the cluster, Pods are automatically added. Used for cluster-level infrastructure services.
example_use: Deploy log collectors (Fluentd), monitoring agents (Prometheus node-exporter), or storage daemons on every node.

description: Creates one or more Pods and ensures that a specified number of them successfully terminate. Tracks successful completions and retries failed Pods.
example_use: Run database migrations, batch data processing, or one-time initialization tasks.

description: Manages time-based Jobs in a cron-like schedule. Creates Jobs according to a defined schedule for recurring tasks.
example_use: Schedule nightly reports, periodic cache cleanup, or daily database backups.

description: An abstract way to expose an application running on a set of Pods as a network service. Types: ClusterIP (internal only, default), NodePort (exposes on each node's IP at a static port), LoadBalancer (provisions an external load balancer via cloud provider).
example_use: Expose a web application internally (ClusterIP), externally on a node port (NodePort), or via cloud load balancer (LoadBalancer).

description: Manages external HTTP/HTTPS access to Services within a cluster, providing URL routing, SSL termination, and virtual hosting. Requires an Ingress controller to function.
example_use: Route traffic from example.com/api to an API service and example.com/app to a frontend service with TLS termination.

description: Represents a class of Ingress resources implemented by a specific controller. Allows multiple Ingress controllers to coexist in the same cluster.
example_use: Define 'nginx' and 'traefik' IngressClasses so different Ingress resources use different controllers.

description: Stores non-confidential configuration data as key-value pairs. Can be consumed by Pods as environment variables, command-line arguments, or configuration files in a volume.
example_use: Store application configuration, environment-specific settings, or configuration files for injection into containers.

description: Stores sensitive information such as passwords, OAuth tokens, and SSH keys. Values are base64-encoded (not encrypted by default without encryption-at-rest configuration).
example_use: Store database credentials, TLS certificates, container registry credentials, or API tokens.

description: A piece of storage in the cluster provisioned by an administrator or dynamically by a StorageClass. Has a lifecycle independent of any individual Pod.
example_use: Represent an NFS share, cloud disk, or local storage available for workloads to claim.

description: A request for storage by a user. Claims can request specific size and access modes. Bound to a matching PersistentVolume or dynamically provisioned via StorageClass.
example_use: Request 10Gi of ReadWriteOnce storage for a database pod.

description: Describes the 'class' of storage offered. Allows dynamic provisioning of PersistentVolumes when a PersistentVolumeClaim is created. Different classes may map to quality-of-service levels or backup policies.
example_use: Define 'fast' (SSD) and 'standard' (HDD) storage classes backed by cloud block storage provisioners.

description: Provides an identity for processes running in a Pod, enabling them to authenticate with the Kubernetes API server. Each namespace has a default ServiceAccount.
example_use: Create a dedicated ServiceAccount for a CI tool with only the permissions it needs to deploy workloads.

description: Defines a set of permissions within a specific namespace. Contains rules that represent a set of permissions on API resources. Always scoped to a single namespace.
example_use: Allow a ServiceAccount to read Pods and ConfigMaps in the 'production' namespace.

description: Like Role but cluster-scoped. Can be used to grant access to non-namespaced resources (Nodes, PersistentVolumes) or across all namespaces.
example_use: Grant cluster-wide read access to all Nodes and PersistentVolumes for a monitoring tool.

description: Grants the permissions defined in a Role to a user, group, or ServiceAccount within a specific namespace. Can bind either a Role or a ClusterRole.
example_use: Bind a developer's user account to the 'pod-reader' Role in the 'staging' namespace.

description: Grants the permissions defined in a ClusterRole to a user, group, or ServiceAccount across the entire cluster.
example_use: Grant a cluster admin ServiceAccount full control over all resources cluster-wide.

description: Automatically scales the number of Pods in a Deployment, ReplicaSet, or StatefulSet based on observed CPU/memory utilization or custom metrics.
example_use: Scale a web service between 2 and 20 replicas based on CPU utilization target of 60%.

description: Automatically adjusts CPU and memory resource requests and limits for containers based on historical usage. Requires the VPA admission controller. Complements HPA.
example_use: Automatically right-size resource requests for a batch processing application with variable load.

description: Limits the number of Pods of a replicated application that can be voluntarily disrupted at the same time during operations like node drains or rolling updates.
example_use: Ensure at least 2 out of 3 replicas remain available during cluster maintenance or rolling updates.

description: Specifies how groups of Pods are allowed to communicate with each other and other network endpoints. Requires a CNI plugin that supports NetworkPolicy (Calico, Cilium, etc.).
example_use: Restrict a database Pod to only accept connections from application Pods in the same namespace.

description: Provides a mechanism for isolating groups of resources within a single cluster. Names of resources must be unique within a namespace but not across namespaces.
example_use: Create separate namespaces for 'development', 'staging', and 'production' environments within one cluster.

description: A worker machine in Kubernetes (VM or physical). Each Node contains the services necessary to run Pods: container runtime, kubelet, and kube-proxy.
example_use: Inspect node resource capacity, add taints to dedicate nodes to specific workloads, or drain nodes for maintenance.

description: Enforces minimum and maximum compute resource usage per Pod, Container, or PersistentVolumeClaim in a namespace. Also sets default resource requests and limits.
example_use: Set default CPU/memory limits for containers in a namespace so unspecified Pods get sensible defaults.

description: Provides constraints that limit aggregate resource consumption per namespace. Can limit total CPU, memory, storage, and number of API objects (Pods, Services, etc.).
example_use: Limit a team's namespace to 10 CPUs, 20Gi memory, and 50 Pods to prevent resource monopolization.

description: Exposes the list of IP addresses and ports of Pods backing a Service. Automatically managed by Kubernetes when a Service has a selector; also used to point Services at external resources.
example_use: Manually create Endpoints for a Service that points to an external database not running in the cluster.

description: A scalable and extensible alternative to Endpoints. Splits endpoint data into multiple smaller slices (default max 100 endpoints each) to improve scalability.
example_use: Automatically used by kube-proxy in large clusters to efficiently track Service endpoints without a single large Endpoints object.

description: Extends the Kubernetes API by defining new custom resource types. Enables operators and controllers to use Kubernetes-native objects for application-specific state.
example_use: Define a 'Database' CRD so a database operator can manage databases as first-class Kubernetes objects.

description: Configures webhooks that intercept API requests and can modify (mutate) objects before they are persisted. Used to inject sidecars, set defaults, or modify resources.
example_use: Automatically inject an Istio sidecar proxy into every Pod created in labeled namespaces.

description: Configures webhooks that intercept API requests and can reject them based on custom validation logic, without modifying the object. Runs after mutating webhooks.
example_use: Enforce that all Pods have required security context settings or that images come from approved registries.

description: Defines a priority level for Pods. Higher priority Pods are scheduled before lower priority ones and can preempt lower priority Pods when cluster resources are scarce.
example_use: Assign 'high-priority' to critical system components so they are scheduled first and can preempt batch jobs.

description: Defines different container runtime configurations available in the cluster. Allows Pods to select a specific runtime (e.g., gVisor, Kata Containers) for enhanced isolation.
example_use: Define a 'gvisor' RuntimeClass so security-sensitive workloads can opt into sandboxed execution.
